step.title: Step 20
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
WOW! Something very interesting happened! After moving RIGHT 6 times:
- The green box moved from (30-32, 12-14) to (30-32, 42-44) - it crossed the purple bar and entered the right panel!
- The green box is now at (30-32, 42-44) with a charcoal dot (c) at (31, 43)
- The middle green box is at (30-32, 27-29) with a white dot (W) at (31, 28)
- The dots SWAPPED when the green box moved! The green box that was at (30-32, 12-14) with W dot is now at (30-32, 42-44) with c dot.
- The middle green box went from c dot to W dot.
Wait, let me re-read. The green box at (30-32, 12-14) had W dot. After moving RIGHT 6 times, there are now TWO green boxes:
1. Green box at (30-32, 27-29) with W dot - this is the middle green box
2. Green box at (30-32, 42-44) with c dot - this is the green box I moved
So the green box I moved went from (30-32, 12-14) to (30-32, 42-44), passing through the middle green box's position. When it passed through, the dots swapped!
Actually wait, looking more carefully:
- Node 6: green box at (30-32, 27-29) with child Node 9 (W dot at 31, 28)
- Node 7: green box at (30-32, 42-44) with child Node 10 (c dot at 31, 43)
So the green box I moved is now at (30-32, 42-44) in the right panel, and it has a charcoal dot. The middle green box at (30-32, 27-29) has a white dot.
The green box is now inside the right panel, near the right charcoal box. The right charcoal box is at (26-30, 44-48). The green box is at (30-32, 42-44), which is to the left and below the charcoal box.
This is very interesting! The green box can cross the purple bar! And when it does, the dots swap.
Now let me think about the goal. The right panel has a charcoal box with a white 3x3 interior. The green box is now in the right panel. Maybe the goal is to move the green box into the right charcoal box?
Let me try moving the green box UP and RIGHT to get it into the right charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48).
The green box is at (30-32, 42-44). The charcoal box is at (26-30, 44-48).
To get the green box inside the charcoal box, I need to move it to (27-29, 45-47).
From (30-32, 42-44), I need to move UP 3 times and RIGHT 1 time.
But wait, the green box is 3x3, so its center is at (31, 43). The charcoal box interior is at (27-29, 45-47), so the green box center should be at (28, 46).
From (31, 43) to (28, 46): UP 3, RIGHT 3.
Actually wait, I need to be careful. The green box at (30-32, 42-44) has its top-left at (30, 42). To fit inside the charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48), the green box's top-left should be at (27, 45).
From (30, 42) to (27, 45): UP 3, RIGHT 3.
But the green box is currently at (30-32, 42-44), which overlaps with the charcoal box's bottom row (row 30). Let me try moving UP and RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# AMAZING! The green box crossed the purple bar!
# Green box moved from (30-32, 12-14) to (30-32, 42-44) - entered right panel!
# Dots swapped: middle green box now has W dot, moved green box has c dot
#
# Green box is now at (30-32, 42-44) in the right panel
# Right charcoal box is at (26-30, 44-48)
# Let me try to move the green box INTO the right charcoal box
# From (30, 42) to (27, 45): UP 3, RIGHT 3

result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print(f"Result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green box: boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
    if n['pixels'] <= 1:
        print(f"Dot: color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        print(f"Charcoal box: boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Result: board_changed=False
Level completed: False
Green box: boundary=[[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]], children=[9]
Green box: boundary=[[30, 42], [30, 44], [32, 44], [32, 42]], children=[10]
Dot: color=W, boundary=[[31, 28]]
Dot: color=c, boundary=[[31, 43]]
Charcoal box: boundary=[[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]], children=[12]
